$MELI COO Daniel Rabinovich operates a ~$90 B company with a contrarian playbook. He rejects standard tech industry advice entirely. He demands brutal honesty, high-risk decisions, and observing actual behavior instead of asking users what they want. I will break down his interview with Aprender de Grandes from nearly 2 years ago.

Rabinovich hates focus groups. He views asking users what they want as a crutch for weak managers. Weak managers use surveys to avoid blame if a product fails. Rabinovich follows a strict rule to not listen to users but observe them. Builders must have the courage to make decisions that cause a little bit of panic.

As companies scale, they usually become slow and corporate. Rabinovich calls the phrase "nobody ever got fired for buying IBM" the ultimate corporate evil. I remember Peter Lynch mocked this phrase too 🤣 It proves that employees care more about avoiding embarrassment than actually winning. He notes that real talent shines during a crisis, where corporate titles vanish and pure competence takes over.

The tech industry loves hyper-specialization. Rabinovich strongly disagrees. He believes that being a narrow specialist is incredibly dangerous. AI will soon commoditize repetitive, highly specialized skills. To survive, workers must be multidisciplinary generalists who can draw creative connections across different fields. MELI is leaning heavily into AI, proving exactly why they need adaptable generalists. They are building their own agentic commerce tools rather than relying on basic 3P software. Their new Mercado Pago AI Assistant already resolves 87% of customer inquiries without human help. Their AI-powered bidding algorithms helped drive a massive 67% growth in their advertising business. They are completely redesigning the shopping experience, and they rely on versatile engineers to pull it off.

Daniel Rabinovich’s approach is focuses on winning by taking bold risks, promoting true builders, and tearing down the corporate safety net.

$MELI stock is down -33% from ATH, largely driven by margin compression. However, it’s important to understand the drivers behind this decline. Management is deliberately reinvesting 5–6 percentage points of margin into free shipping expansion, first-party inventory, cross-border trade, and credit card growth. This creates short-term pressure, but structurally strengthens MercadoLibre’s competitive position.

MELI is building consumer trust through free shipping and acquiring fintech users at low cost via its marketplace funnel.

Latin America’s e-commerce market is projected to exceed $215B by 2026, growing ~1.5x faster than the global average, while penetration remains relatively low. MercadoLibre dominates Brazil, Mexico, and Argentina, reinforcing its leadership.

Gross merchandise volume grew 35% in Q4 2025, with 100M+ unique annual buyers. Its logistics network, Mercado Envíos, delivers 49% of orders same-day or next-day, making logistics a key competitive moat.

Mercado Pago is scaling beyond the core platform. Off-platform TPV is growing faster than on-platform, confirming that fintech has independent momentum.

The credit business is expanding rapidly. The portfolio grew +90% YoY, while the >90-day delinquency ratio declined to 17%, indicating improving credit quality alongside growth.

The real advantage lies in platform synergy: a user discovers a product, pays via Mercado Pago, uses credit financing, receives delivery via Mercado Envíos, and stores funds in a yield-bearing account. No competitor in the region offers this level of integration.
